name: frozendict (Immutable Dictionary)

performance.notes: C extension in frozendict 2.x; slightly faster than pure dict for reads

agent_readiness.notes: Immutable dict implementation. from frozendict import frozendict. d = frozendict({'a': 1, 'b': 2}). d['a'] → 1. hash(d) → stable hash (hashable for use as dict key or in sets). d.set('c', 3) → new frozendict({'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}). d.delete('a') → new frozendict({'b': 2}). d.update({'c': 3}) → new frozendict. frozendict is a subclass of dict — works with isinstance(d, dict). Supports all read-only dict methods. Used as keyword arguments, caching keys, immutable configs. {d: 'value'} in another dict works. frozendict({}).copy() → frozendict. Persistent data structure via copy-on-write operations.

gotchas: Values must be hashable for frozendict itself to be hashable — nested mutable objects break hashing
frozendict is a dict subclass — some code may mutate it if they bypass the API
set() / delete() return new frozendict — not in-place mutation
Pure Python fallback in older versions — C extension only in frozendict 2.x+
best_when: Using dicts as dictionary keys or set members (requires hashability)
Immutable configuration objects that should never be mutated
Thread-safe shared state where dict mutation would cause race conditions
Functional programming patterns needing persistent maps
avoid_when: When types.MappingProxyType is sufficient (stdlib, no dep)
When nested mutable values exist (hash breaks)
summary: frozendict provides an immutable, hashable Python dictionary. frozendict({'a': 1}). hash(d) works. d.set('b', 2) returns new frozendict. Dict subclass — isinstance(d, dict). MIT/LGPL-3.0 by Marco Sulla. C extension in v2.x. Best for hashable dict keys, immutable configs, thread-safe state. Values must be hashable for dict to be hashable. set()/delete() are copy-on-write. types.MappingProxyType for simpler stdlib alternative.
